title: Using AI-assisted coding to build software prototypes is an important way to quickly explore many ideas and invent new things. In this and future posts, I’d like to share with you some best practices… | Andrew Ng | 292 comments
author: Andrew Ng
text:
Using AI-assisted coding to build software prototypes is an important way to quickly explore many ideas and invent new things. In this and future posts, I’d like to share with you some best practices for prototyping simple web apps. This post will focus on one idea: being opinionated about the software stack. The software stack I personally use changes every few weeks. There are many good alternatives to these choices, and if you pick a preferred software stack and become familiar with its components, you’ll be able to develop more quickly. But as an illustration, here’s my current default: - Python with FastAPI for building web-hosted APIs: I develop primarily in Python, so that’s a natural choice for me. If you’re a JavaScript/TypeScript developer, you’ll likely make a different choice. I’ve found FastAPI really easy to use and scalable for deploying web services (APIs) hosted in Python. - Uvicorn to run the backend application server (to execute code and serve web pages) for local testing on my laptop. - If deploying on the cloud, then either Heroku for small apps or AWS Elastic Beanstalk for larger ones (disclosure: I serve on Amazon’s board of directors): There are many services for deploying jobs, including HuggingFace Spaces, Railway, Google’s Firebase, Vercel, and others. Many of these work fine, and becoming familiar with just 1 or 2 will simplify your development process. - MongoDB for NoSQL database: While traditional SQL databases are amazing feats of engineering that result in highly efficient and reliable data storage, the need to define the database structure (or schema) slows down prototyping. If you really need speed and ease of implementation, then dumping most of your data into a NoSQL (unstructured or semi-structured) database such as MongoDB lets you write code quickly and sort out later exactly what you want to do with the data. This is sometimes called schema-on-write, as opposed to schema-on-read. Mind you, if an application goes to scaled production, there are many use cases where a more structured SQL database is significantly more reliable and scalable. - OpenAI’s o1 and Anthropic’s Claude 3.5 Sonnet for coding assistance, often by prompting directly (when operating at the conceptual/design level). Also occasionally Cursor (when operating at the code level). I hope never to have to code again without AI assistance! Claude 3.5 Sonnet is widely regarded as one of the best coding models. And o1 is incredible at planning and building more complex software modules, but you do have to learn to prompt it differently. On top of all this, of course, I use many AI tools to manage agentic workflows, data ingestion, retrieval augmented generation, and so on. DeepLearning.AI and our wonderful partners offer courses on many of these tools. [Reached length limit; full text: https://lnkd.in/gCY5N5sA ]
Andrew Ng, really resonated with your post about AI-assisted coding and prototyping best practices. I couldn't help but write the following: Seeing how you've integrated various AI tools into your workflow reminded me exactly why we built GoCodeo in the first place. While tools like Claude and o1 are fantastic for conceptual planning and coding assistance, one crucial aspect often gets overlooked- testing. That's where GoCodeo comes in. We've built an AI collaborator that transforms the entire development process by seamlessly integrating testing throughout your workflow, not just at the end. What excites me most about your stack is how our AI coding & testing agent could enhance it. Imagine having an AI system that doesn't just help you write code, but also: - Automatically generates tests as you code. - Predicts potential issues before they arise. - Helps maintain code quality throughout the rapid prototyping phase. And since you mentioned never wanting to code without AI assistance again (I completely agree!), I'd love to show how GoCodeo could complement your existing AI toolkit, especially when working with FastAPI and Python.
Great advice Andrew Ng however, I think While adapting the stack every few weeks could be counterproductive for many developers. Constantly switching tools can lead to a lack of deep mastery and efficiency. For teams or less experienced developers, sticking to a consistent stack for longer periods might be more practical. The heavy emphasis on AI-assisted coding, while innovative, may create dependencies that are problematic for developers who don’t fully understand the underlying code or concepts. Relying too much on tools like OpenAI and Claude without building foundational skills could hinder a developer’s ability to debug or extend complex systems. While MongoDB is a great choice for rapid development, the assumption that NoSQL databases are always faster for prototyping might not hold true in every scenario. Many modern SQL databases, such as SQLite or PostgreSQL, offer schema flexibility and rapid iteration capabilities, which can rival NoSQL for many use cases. While AWS Elastic Beanstalk and Heroku are solid recommendations, they are not the most cost-effective or user-friendly options for smaller developers or startups. alternatives like Vercel or Railway in greater detail could provide more balanced guidance.
Andrew Ng , Completely agree that adopting AI assisted coding is critical for quickly exploring many ideas (or for doing any software development in the future for that matter), and we should be opinionated about the software stack that we pick to move quickly forward. Also, thanks for your recommendation on the software stack. Most of it resonates with me - from OpenAI’s o1 for higher level planning of complex software development and Claude 3.5 Sonnet for actual coding to Python and FastAPI. But I think a lot depends on your overall technical environment though. For instance, if you work for a company or client who are on Azure, then on the NOSql side, cosmos DB is also a pretty good choice, rather than pure Mongo DB. I understand OpenAI uses Cosmos DB as part of their software stack. Similarly if the company or client is on GCP, you may not have much choice about using OpenAI o1. You will probably have to use a combination of Gemini 2.0 and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. Although Gemini may be still lagging a bit behind o1, I am impressed with some of the products built on top of that stack, such as NotebookLM. Having said all that, yes, it is important to get agreement among the team on the software stack early in the project.
I fully agree and endorse this piece. I have had a very similar experience with AI assisted coding and found that Claude Sonnet 3.5 is probably the best model out there for rapid prototyping and code generation. A lot of companies are forcing their employees to stick to a particular tool, which in my opinion defies the whole purpose. These tools come down to personal experience and preference, as each person prompts differently and a particular tool may be good at exactly that. Companies should encourage employees to explore and use the tools, and choose the tool that works for them best! I personally love and use almost the exact same stack as Andrew here, highly recommended read!
Lovable is amazing for prototyping React apps - you don’t need coding experience and it integrates to GitHub so you have the full codebase. This is a game changer for removing silos between teams. Andrew Ng I would also think about data structures sooner rather than later - too often the problems in codebases arise from poorly designed data layers leading to complex code trying to compensate. In other words prototype with noSQL but identify use cases for strong schema early on.
I agree, Sonnet is very powerful in translating very high level and confused requirements into a working code. For me UIs have always been big rocks and now I can just whisper what the backend would be and get the UI done in 60 seconds or less.
Totally agree - With AI assisted coding, we will see a faster idea-to-validation cycle. And AI have also release a massive potential for entrepreneurs with less tech flare to test prototyping/MVP faster and cheaper with no-code platforms like webflow, bubble and zapier. This in interesting because we will most likely end up scaling ideas that have greater chance of surviving.
